# Appendix — The Lab Environment {.unnumbered}

> This is the **only** part of the book tied to a specific set of tools. The chapters teach
> concepts and stay tool-agnostic on purpose, so that when the environment changes, only this
> page does.

The practical work happens in the labs, not in this book. Each chapter ends with an **In the
lab** note describing the *capability* you'll exercise (for example, "crack real password
hashes"); this appendix says *how* — which environment and which tools — for the current
offering.

## This offering

*(Coordinator: keep this section current. It is the single source of environment truth.)*

Practical work runs in a provided **virtual machine** with the tools pre-installed. Set it up
in Week 1 (see the Week 1 lab). You do not install tools yourself; you use the environment you
are given.

## Capability → tool map

The concepts in each chapter are exercised with these tools. Tools change between offerings;
the concept does not.

| Capability (from the chapter) | Current tool(s) |
|-------------------------------|-----------------|
| Scan a host for open ports and services | Nmap / Zenmap |
| Crack password hashes; run a dictionary attack | (password-cracking tools) |
| Capture and read network traffic | Wireshark |
| Encrypt / decrypt; generate keys | web crypto tools; PGP |
| Configure and test a firewall | (network/firewall tool) |
| Recover and analyse forensic evidence | (forensics toolkit) |

## A note on where this is heading

The environment is expected to move from the current VM to **container-based labs** (Docker),
which are lighter, reproducible, one-command to launch, and closer to how security work is
done in industry. Because the chapters name *capabilities* rather than tools, that migration
will not change a word of them — only this appendix and the lab worksheets. If you are reading
this in a later offering, trust the table above over any tool named in passing elsewhere.
